reference: GATE CS — Computer Networks / Network Architecture

map.condition: Need to find MAC address from known IP address
Need to find IP address from known MAC address
Packet too large for next link (fragmentation)
Identify which layer handles a given function (routing / framing / reliability)

map.use: \text{DNS, DHCP, SNMP, TFTP} \to \text{UDP};\quad \text{HTTP, FTP, SMTP, Telnet, SSH} \to \text{TCP}

formulas.name: OSI Layer Order (bottom to top)
formulas.plain: 1. Physical | 2. Data Link | 3. Network | 4. Transport | 5. Session | 6. Presentation | 7. Application
formulas.whenToUse: When identifying which OSI layer a given function, protocol, or device belongs to.

terms.means: Protocol Data Unit — the name of data at each layer (bits / frames / packets / segments / data)

formulas.trap: Confusing Physical (bit transmission) with Data Link (framing, MAC, error detection on a single hop) — switches operate at Layer 2, routers at Layer 3.

formulas.name: OSI Layer Key Functions
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{Layer} & \text{Function} \\ \hline 2\,(DLL) & \text{Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (CRC), flow control on link} \\ 3\,(Network) & \text{Logical addressing (IP), routing, packet forwarding} \\ 4\,(Transport) & \text{End-to-end delivery, port addressing, segmentation, e2e error/flow control} \\ 5\,(Session) & \text{Session establishment, synchronisation, dialogue control} \\ 6\,(Presentation) & \text{Encryption, compression, data format translation} \\ 7\,(Application) & \text{User-facing protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS} \end{array}
formulas.plain: DLL: framing/MAC/CRC | Network: IP routing | Transport: end-to-end, ports | Session: sync | Presentation: encrypt/compress | Application: HTTP/DNS/FTP
formulas.whenToUse: When matching a layer name to its primary responsibility in GATE matching questions.

terms.means: Data Link Layer (Layer 2)
End-to-end (across entire network path)

formulas.trap: Transport layer does NOT route packets — it provides end-to-end reliability. Routing is exclusively Layer 3 (Network layer).

formulas.name: PDU Names per Layer
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks what data is called at a specific layer.

terms.means: Protocol Data Unit

formulas.trap: UDP units are called datagrams, not segments. IP units are packets (not datagrams, though IP datagrams is also accepted).

formulas.name: TCP/IP 4-Layer Model
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing TCP/IP model to OSI model or locating a protocol within TCP/IP.

terms.means: Internet Protocol — handles logical addressing and routing

formulas.trap: TCP/IP has 4 layers, not 7. OSI layers 5,6,7 all map to the single Application layer in TCP/IP.

formulas.name: OSI to TCP/IP Layer Mapping
formulas.plain: OSI 7+6+5 -> Application; OSI 4 -> Transport; OSI 3 -> Internet; OSI 2+1 -> Network Access
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks which TCP/IP layer corresponds to a given OSI layer or function.
formulas.trap: There is no Session or Presentation layer in TCP/IP — those functions (encryption, session control) fall inside the Application layer.

formulas.name: Standard Header Field Bit Widths
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{Field} & \text{Size (bits)} \\ \hline \text{UDP / TCP Port Number} & 16 \\ \text{TCP Sequence Number} & 32 \\ \text{TCP Acknowledgment Number} & 32 \\ \text{Ethernet MAC Address} & 48 \\ \text{IPv4 Address} & 32 \\ \text{IPv6 Address} & 128 \\ \text{IPv6 Next Header} & 8 \\ \text{IPv4 Time-to-Live (TTL)} & 8 \end{array}
formulas.plain: UDP/TCP Port=16b, TCP Seq#=32b, TCP Ack#=32b, MAC=48b, IPv4=32b, IPv6=128b, IPv6 NextHdr=8b, TTL=8b
formulas.whenToUse: When a GATE matching question asks for the bit length of specific header fields.

terms.means: Time To Live — hop count field in IPv4
IPv6 field indicating the type of the next header/extension

formulas.trap: Port numbers are 16 bits (not 32). MAC addresses are 48 bits (6 bytes), NOT 32 bits like IPv4 addresses.

formulas.name: Standard IPv4 / UDP / TCP Header Sizes
formulas.latex: H_{IPv4} = 20\text{ bytes (min)},\quad H_{UDP} = 8\text{ bytes},\quad H_{TCP} = 20\text{ bytes (min)}
formulas.plain: IPv4 header = 20 bytes (min), UDP header = 8 bytes, TCP header = 20 bytes (min)
formulas.whenToUse: When calculating data payload, fragmentation offsets, or effective throughput.

terms.means: IPv4 header size

formulas.trap: UDP header is only 8 bytes (source port 2, dest port 2, length 2, checksum 2). TCP minimum header is 20 bytes.

formulas.name: Number of Fragments
formulas.whenToUse: When a datagram with payload D bytes must be sent over a link with a given MTU.

terms.means: Data payload size in bytes (original IP datagram size minus IP header)
Maximum Transmission Unit of the link
IP header size (typically 20 bytes)

formulas.trap: Each fragment also carries its own 20-byte IP header, so the usable data per fragment is (MTU - 20). Fragmentation offset is in units of 8 bytes.

formulas.name: Fragment Offset Field
formulas.plain: Offset of k-th fragment = (sum of data bytes in previous fragments) / 8
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the fragment offset field value for each IP fragment.

terms.means: Data bytes carried by the i-th fragment

formulas.trap: Offset is measured in 8-byte units (not bytes). Each fragment's data size must itself be a multiple of 8, except the last fragment.

formulas.name: ARP — Address Resolution Protocol
formulas.plain: ARP: IP Address -> MAC Address (broadcast query on local LAN)
formulas.whenToUse: When a host knows the destination IP but needs the Layer 2 MAC address to deliver a frame on the local network.

terms.means: Address Resolution Protocol — maps IP to MAC on a local subnet

formulas.trap: ARP resolves IP to MAC (not the reverse). It works only within a single subnet/broadcast domain. ARP is NOT used to find the IP of a domain name — that is DNS.

formulas.name: RARP, DNS, DHCP Functions
formulas.plain: RARP: MAC->IP | DNS: hostname->IP | DHCP: dynamically assigns IP address
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks which protocol performs a specific address-lookup or assignment function.

terms.means: Reverse ARP — maps MAC to IP (legacy; replaced by DHCP)
Domain Name System — resolves hostnames to IP addresses
Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol — auto-assigns IP, mask, gateway

formulas.trap: ARP is NOT for finding the IP address corresponding to a MAC — that is RARP. ARP goes the other direction (IP to MAC).

groups.groupTitle: TCP vs UDP & Protocol Transport Mapping

formulas.name: TCP vs UDP Properties
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{lll} & \text{TCP} & \text{UDP} \\ \hline \text{Connection} & \text{Connection-oriented} & \text{Connectionless} \\ \text{Reliability} & \text{Reliable (ACK, retransmit)} & \text{Unreliable} \\ \text{Order} & \text{In-order delivery} & \text{No ordering} \\ \text{Flow control} & \text{Yes (window)} & \text{No} \\ \text{Overhead} & \text{High (20B header)} & \text{Low (8B header)} \end{array}
formulas.plain: TCP: connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, flow-controlled, 20B header. UDP: connectionless, unreliable, unordered, 8B header.
formulas.whenToUse: When determining whether a scenario requires TCP or UDP, or comparing their properties.

terms.means: Acknowledgement — TCP uses ACKs to confirm receipt

formulas.trap: TCP provides end-to-end reliability; if the transport protocol is UDP, the application layer must handle its own reliability (e.g., TFTP does this at application level).

formulas.name: Application Protocol to Transport Mapping
formulas.latex: \underbrace{\text{DNS, DHCP, SNMP, TFTP, RIP}}_{\text{UDP}} \qquad \underbrace{\text{HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, Telnet, SSH, POP3, IMAP}}_{\text{TCP}}
formulas.plain: UDP: DNS, DHCP, SNMP, TFTP, RIP | TCP: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, Telnet, SSH, POP3, IMAP
formulas.whenToUse: When a GATE question asks which application protocol uses UDP or TCP.

terms.means: Simple Network Management Protocol — uses UDP port 161
Trivial FTP — uses UDP (adds its own reliability at app layer)

formulas.trap: DNS primarily uses UDP (port 53) for queries but uses TCP for zone transfers. DHCP uses UDP (ports 67/68). A common trap is placing DNS under TCP only.

formulas.name: Well-Known Port Numbers
formulas.plain: FTP:20/21, SSH:22, Telnet:23, SMTP:25, DNS:53, HTTP:80, HTTPS:443, DHCP:67/68
formulas.whenToUse: When a question tests recognition of port numbers for standard application protocols.

terms.means: 16-bit number identifying an application endpoint at the transport layer

formulas.trap: FTP uses TWO ports: 21 for control (commands) and 20 for data transfer. SSH replaces Telnet (port 23) with encrypted communication on port 22.

formulas.name: Statistical TDM Throughput
formulas.whenToUse: When computing how much of the channel capacity is utilised in statistical TDM with bursty sources.

terms.means: Number of sources actually transmitting in a given time slot

formulas.trap: Statistical TDM allocates slots dynamically, so efficiency depends on actual traffic load, not the total number of sources.

formulas.name: Polling Channel Throughput
formulas.plain: Efficiency = T_tx / (T_tx + T_poll)   where T_poll = polling overhead per node
formulas.whenToUse: When a broadcast channel uses polling and you need effective throughput or efficiency.

terms.means: Time to transmit one packet
Polling delay between consecutive node polls

formulas.trap: Polling overhead reduces throughput even when multiple nodes always have data. Total capacity used = N * (T_tx + T_poll) per cycle for N nodes.

formulas.name: What Transport Must Add Over Network Layer
formulas.plain: Transport adds port-based multiplexing (always); TCP adds reliability + ordering + flow control on top of IP.
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks which functionalities MUST be implemented by transport over and above the network layer.

terms.means: Identifying the correct application using port numbers

formulas.trap: Duplicate packet detection and in-order delivery are NOT guaranteed by IP (network layer) — they must be added by TCP (transport layer) if needed. UDP does not add these.

formulas.name: Encapsulation / Decapsulation
formulas.plain: App data + transport header = Segment; + network header = Packet; + DL header = Frame; -> bits
formulas.whenToUse: When tracing how data is wrapped with headers as it passes down the protocol stack.

terms.means: Transport layer header (TCP/UDP)
Network layer header (IP)
Data Link layer header (Ethernet frame header)

formulas.trap: At each intermediate router, only the IP header (and below) is read; the TCP/UDP header is NOT examined by routers unless doing deep packet inspection.
